speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Clay Gustin grew up on a wood ranch in the foothills of Moscow Mountain. He worked as a young man in Moscow at various jobs. In this interview, he discusses the park's sleigh hall, fatal accidents in the woods, the McGarry Butte fire, advantages of horse logging,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: the few moonshine stories. The first thing I thought I'd ask you about the ranch on the wood ranch on the Coomerun Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were just a kid there, huh?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: That's where you were born.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I was born right before the mountain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when that, was it just a wood ranch? I mean, it's just for cutting wood and there's no farming at all?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No farming.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No. We just had a wood ranch up there. Dad worked out in harvest, you know, during harvest. We went to school there at the end of the mountain. No, we didn't have a farm. All we'd done up in there was cut wood in the wintertime and then we'd haul it in the summer, you know, haul it to Moscow.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: For firewood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you have to go to go to school?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: It was about three miles from there down, they called it the Evergreen School, just part of the Moscow Mountains there before you start into them. It's still there, I guess. I haven't been out there for years. Oh, there's quite a bunch of kids went to school there.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: There's Becks. I seen him the other day. He's talking about the school. He's about my new school.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: You had a lot of brothers and sisters.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, I had, let me see, I think there's six boys and four girls.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They're all dead except two or three of us, one girl and my brother in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have many neighbors out there or was it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I was a little around through the country there at that time when we was kids.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I can't think of all the names. There's some Coles lived there, Gid Cole,

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Erikson, Dahlberg, and there's some other people the name of Stratton, Homer Burr's family,

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Haddon, Coopers, and Hart. Oh, there's quite a bunch of them. That isn't about, you know, two or three miles around the country there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they wood ranches too or were they fires?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, most of them was in the timber. I guess some of them had little land cleared and stuff like that to farm on, raise hay for the stock. It wasn't really farmers, but then after you got a little further down, then it was all farmers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how your father came about getting the place?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No, I don't. He had it on out of... He probably bought it. He didn't. Homesteaded. No, I don't think he homesteaded. I think he bought it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the timber still on it when you were a kid?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: It was still there when he got rid of it except what we cut for wood. I see they've logged it though since then. I don't know, maybe several times. Well, how old were you when you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you remember starting to work out there and cutting, helping, right?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, it was about time after I got going to school, you know, as kids we worked all the time when we were home. Soon as we got big enough to hang on to the saw, we was cutting wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the operation like there?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I figure it was really small.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I mean, just with a family. Well, we'd just go out there and they'd fall the tree and we'd saw them in the four -foot blocks. Then the bigger ones, they'd split them. God, I don't remember when it did start, but I remember being there, you know, from... growed up. I was about 14 years old. Well, we moved to Moscow there. I went to school there for a little while, and then I quit school and took off up there and went and been here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything about Evergreen School, what it was like going there?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I remember when the teachers used to bounce you around on the seats to make you mine.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I seen them pick them up and set them down there quite a few times.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: It's a lot different than it is now. They used to make you mine, but now they don't dare to. They'd hang the teacher. They'd take some of the kids. No, I don't... It's been so long ago, I forgot. I know there used to be some great big kids there, you know, being, I suppose, sixth, seventh grade. They used to... The teachers, they'd hold their hand up and want something, you know, just for meaningless. She had a big platform up there, and she'd make them come up there and lay down on the... to punish them. I remember a lot of times, they had that thing full of them. They'd just do it for honest reasons. They wanted something, and then when she'd ask them what they wanted, they didn't know what it was. She'd take them up there. That's the way she... They'd stand in the corner someplace.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But they was having grown men in there at them days, you know, trying to get an education. Didn't want one either, I guess. Didn't act like it.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I mean, they hadn't had a chance to go to school before that, and they wanted to go and learn.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, we didn't get to... We'd suffer from school there. You know, when the snow gets so deep, sometimes you wouldn't get to school.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But you used to have to hike, and by the time you get there, you'd be late. There wasn't no set time. We were supposed to have been there on time, but we couldn't get there. It didn't seem to make much difference as long as you got there and back. That was the main thing. What kind of house did you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know, it was just a little log cabin?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No, it was a lumber house they'd built. It ain't there no more. It wasn't the last time I was in that country. They'd logged it, and they'd tore down everything that was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get along pretty good with all that brothers and sisters?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, yeah. They'd fight once in a while, but that didn't make any difference. No, they had a pretty good house there at some time.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Then my mother's folks, they lived there too. They lived up in the little boughs in there. Her brother and her mother. Of course, they're all dead now, a whole bunch of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do in Moscow when you went there? What were the jobs? What was it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, I drove the drey team there for Williamson for a while. Then I worked on this paving the streets there. You know, had a vat down there to cook oil in. I worked at that. Then I worked in that harvester there at Moscow. And they had the harvester there where that big store is, out on North Main. The Rosauers. Rosauers. Worked in there. Then when I was going to school, I used to split wood for them. That fella used to run the penny store. You know, split it into cook, wood firm. Had all kinds of jobs around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you pull on the drey team? What did you deliver?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, we delivered coal and trunks and furniture. I didn't get in on much of that furniture because I was too rough with it. They didn't like that. I didn't like the mood. Them old ladies would stand out there with a broom and hit you over the head if you scratched something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the coal? Where did you deliver that?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I delivered that up at the university. A lot of it. And then around town, you know, different ones. They'd order it and you'd have to take it out. But the university had most of it. Flat car gondolas come in there and we'd load them out of them, shovel it out, take it up there, and dump it into that furnace to keep that heating plant going. They could just about burn a load while I was getting one too.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Then they finally put the railroad up there. I don't know. They ain't got the railroad there now. I don't know what the hell they get it up there. Maybe they don't use coal anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me that you delivered flour too?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, to the bakeries. That's the first job I had of a morning. Go down there and get about 10 sacks, 100 pound sacks of flour and take it up to that Third Street bakery. I forget who owned that now. I knew him well then too. It's the same building. Yeah, same place. But I can't think of the color of his name. It was run with them. Then I'd go overnight the last thing and get that bread and deliver it out there where that road is. And then take a bunch of it to the university up there. They had two places for them. Boy, there's a lot of bread and a thousand pounds of that flour when you get it mixed up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it, the Rose Hours place?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They had an army camp there at the time. And one at the university.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you take it to other stores too to sell?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Just those two places. Them two places. The only place I'd been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the army camp like?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I didn't know that there was one. Oh, this mess hall and stuff like that where the pheasant went there. Big long building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then you worked later in that harvester plant on that same site?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: That was before that I worked in there. They got that for the army out there.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Used to haul scrap iron out there and they'd melt it. We'd pour it into these molds. That was a hot job too.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Gov. Bixley was in on that I think. He was lieutenant governor later on. I don't think he was at the time. Later on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it such a hot job? Was it a foundry?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, that iron, you know, you had to catch it in them kittles. And then pack it around the port and use your molds they had to fix it for you.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: You could sweat pretty easy in there without doing any work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they make the molds out of?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They used sand mostly. Some kind of sand. They'd make the molds whatever they wanted to make. And they'd pour this stuff in and let it set. Cool it off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know if they used harvesters right around here in this area?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They used them there at Moscow. I don't know. I don't think they used them too long or stood up too long. They used to make these car wheels for these railroads.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: So you came out, you left Moscow pretty young. I left there when I was 14, come up here and then I went back different times and worked there in Moscow. When there wasn't nothing around the woods too much to go back down there and get a job. Stay a while and then I'd take back to the woods. I never did. I don't know. I never did like a town. I'd rather be out here.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: So when you came out here you were still just a punk kid but you weren't all green about logging. I'd been in the woods all my life. I knew about the woods. If I could tell one tree from another I'd know that. Yeah. Oh, I swamped. When I first came down here, along the railroad track, an old feller was with me. We worked together there quite a while. Went sawing and stuff like that. Got on them high lines. Just kept moving around wherever they'd job them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what they first had you do? They had you swamping at the start?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, I was swamping down here at Camp 7. They'd fall in trees along the right -hand side and saw them up and then we'd go on and cut the limbs off.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: And then the motors came in and loaded them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the Beals Butte fire?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: That was in 1914, which I guess was the same.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 1910, wasn't it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I thought it was a little bit later. I thought it was after the... I know this one over here because I was on it. The McGarry Mountain.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: That was quite a bit later.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, that was after they built the railroad in the park over there. We was working at the camp. Camp 1 over there at the time. The fire was starting to put us out on this. But I don't remember this one here. It must have been 31 or 32.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that fire like? The McGarry fire? Huh? What was the McGarry fire like?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: It was pretty hot. It burnt that whole mountain back here. We had to stop down at the river in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do on the fire?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Make trails. I had a crew out there myself making trails. Had to watch them all the time. See where they were at and whether they stayed out of the fire or not. And see if they got their lunches at noon.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Then we backfired from the river back the other way when it got coming down over the hill there because I figured it jumped across on this side. So we'd go along the other side and backfire and let it run up into there. I know we went up Boulder Creek in there one time, a bunch of us, and we were going to put a trail in. Heck, that fire was going clear over us. You know, and lighting down. So we had to take them all out of there then. Then we went back to the river and started backfiring the other way. They'd have stayed in there. That fire got blown there. They'd have had an awful time getting out of there. And they had a lot of green kids on us. They'd have been brought out from Moscow. They didn't know what a fire was or what to do. You just had to watch them all the time.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: If you got lost in the brush, you might have got burned up. But most of them were pretty good workers. They'd do what they told them to do.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, I don't know. Them fires, I don't think much of them. I still think they said it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who said it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I always will think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What? For what?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Say it burns, Royce. Cheaper that way than it would be to hire crews to go out and burn, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they thought it was going to get out of hand like it did?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, it did get out of hand, but I think they said it in the first place. I couldn't swear for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the fire over by Floodwood? Were you on that one?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any others that you fought?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I was on one at Princeton one time down here. A bunch of us went down there to fight that. I found out that one was set too, and I didn't do much fire fighting either. I watched some of them skidways so they wouldn't catch fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Set by who?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: The company.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, it seems like that happens. The Forest Service, they'll set them now. And when they're burning and brushing now, they'll burn off another 50 acres or so.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: It's called a slop over. Yeah, they get away from them and they holler because somebody else set it. But they used to have to pile that brush and burn it, and it would cost a lot of money to get that burnt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean in the old days?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, when they had to pile it all, and they'd get a fire going like this and they'd get burnt pretty cheap. But putting the fire out after it got the brush and burn it. Some of them get away from them and they burn up a lot of timber. That 1910 fire, I wasn't up here then. But that burnt, oh, I don't know, all over. It burnt Bovill up, I guess. Butte, Butte, all through that country. I don't know how much further. A lot of people got burnt up in that, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Bovill a pretty rough town back there?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No, I don't think it was too rough. Clarkia was a rougher town, I think, than Bovill, I bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like in Clarkia?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, they had all kinds of things going over there. Tootle -Eggin', I don't know.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I've heard about that around here, too. Yeah, they've had some of that here, too, I guess.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Not too much of it, as I even know of. Of course, they can get bootlegged whiskey in here, but they hauled it in, I guess. First I'm making it, I don't think too many made it.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No, I forget half of what used to go on around here.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, it's been a pretty long time.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, there are never things about what's going to happen later on or nothing, you know.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Heck, that stuff's 50 years ago that we're talking about.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I know dairy was a lot different when I first came up here. Old shacks and I don't know. Now they've got it fixed up pretty good. They used to have saloons. They used to have one right down the road here. That's Russ Lawrence, I showed you that picture. He was on a saloon right down here on the road when they built this railroad.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: That was before my time when they built the railroad, but he had it after I came up here, too.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I guess it's kind of rough on the one that built the railroad in here, kind of tough on some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you mean?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Building the railroad, they'd get drunk and some of them would get killed and everything. They had most of them bow hunts, they called them. On the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that in the camps it was mostly foreigners that were working the camps when you were first out here? Was it mostly regular?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, it was just about half and half, I'd say. Maybe more of them were foreigners. Because they come in here and they built the camps and then they built railroads all over for the potlatch. No fella had a whole camp full of them guys.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Foreigners, I mean. Of course, they were good railroad workers. About all they knew, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would you say that when the labor activity started, changed conditions that came mostly from them, or did it come as much from the guys that lived around here?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, when it first started, I think there was more of them WAPs in on it. But afterwards, I think it got, well, the rest of them was in on it, too. That's what they gathered up with them WAPs when they come in after them that time. WABL, they called them. Afterwards, there's quite a few of the other guys got in on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the other guys, I mean, the guys that lived around here?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, it worked for the potlatch.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They'd get up for more money and different things.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: And then the potlatch had a union of their own, four Ls. Oh, another union, I forget what they called that. But the potlatch wrote it and you lived up to it.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: So it didn't help you, any?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: You done this what they want you to do, or you didn't stay. No, the union wouldn't stick up for you, so it must have been the potlatch that wrote it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many guys join that one?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, you had to join if you worked, you know. They wouldn't let you work unless you belonged to it. Finally, it got to them. Everybody found out it was a potlatch union, so they finally quit paying in on it and they still stayed and worked. And then I guess that other union, CIO or AFL got in. And that was a little different. They'd take your money and it wouldn't get you no more wages either.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But it wasn't a company. No, it wasn't a company, usually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about the IWW? You weren't supposed to belong to that one, right?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I mean, the company didn't like that. I didn't belong to it. I worked with them guys, though. Never had a card, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you belong to any of them?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Not now. I joined that potlatch there, paid dues in for a long time, and finally I quit that. They said they can't, you know. I said, I don't let them care.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was looking for a job when I got this, and so what's the difference?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I never did pay no more. And I never got canned either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about the strikes that went on when the IWW was up there? Were there strikes?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, they'd strike. I don't know whether the Wobblies would do something and they'd blame it on the Wobblies. I don't think half of them Wobblies done what they claimed they did myself. I think it was somebody else was the head of it. But I know they come up and got a bunch of them up there one time when I was working with them, just on the landing there. They got what had cards around there. Took them to Moscow. I don't know what they done. Kept them with a bull pen down there for a while. I don't know what they ever done with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could they tell if you had a card?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, you'd pack your card with you, you know. If you belonged to the Wobblies. You had a Wobbly card.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they'd search it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I suppose they'd go into them bunk houses. If you had a card laying around or any place, they'd find it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were most of these guys that were taken in?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They had most of them Wops that drowned here at that time. That's about all they picked on, I know. Up there.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But I don't remember much that they'd done to cause them to do it. I guess they'd set a fire or something someplace. Something like that. Somebody would and they'd lay it on to them.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I've heard that it said that they really did get the better conditions. Well, they did do.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Hadn't been for them. I don't think they'd had any bunk houses and bedding and stuff. You'd have to pack your own beds and stuff like that in them camps. You got better bunk houses and better beds and beds furnished. Washrooms and stuff like that.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, them camps was pretty good when I left them. They had everything you'd want. If you'd stand in them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think these bad things were being laid on the IWW?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, they had to blame somebody. I suppose they figured that anything went wrong with the Wobblies doing it.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I worked with them up there and they were all good workers. They used to like to play dice. As soon as there was anything, there was no log there. They were all out there shaking dice. Taking the other guy's money so that they could get it. He used to laugh at them. One time they'd lay a coat out and shake dice and bet. One guy would win a lot of money and probably the next time they tried to eat or lose, the other guys would get it. That's the way they passed away their time while they were busy. They all seemed to have money. Of course, money them days didn't amount to much.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I think it was only about 60 cents an hour. We were working on the landing at that time. But the sleigh hall here, I worked on that for 30 cents an hour. You live just as good as you do now for seven or eight dollars an hour, I guess. I don't know how much that does.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to tell me what that sleigh hall was like?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, about the only thing I could tell you would be how they'd done it. They loaded them on the sleigh on the other end of the line. They brought them about, I'd say, five miles to the river.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They'd bring two sleighs at a time behind each cat over to this railroad. Then they had a loader there, they'd load it on to them cars and take them into potlatch for the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I was road -moaking down here. I saw the logs over there at first, they went to sleigh on, and I went on the road.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Salmon.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They put in 72 days study. Never missed Sundays and all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do to maintain that road? What did you have to do?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, we put sand and gravel on the road to keep the sleighs running over the horses. They had four horses on each sleigh. There was quite a bit of weight there. And them sleighs. Just to hold the sled back down the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to work on it every day?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, yeah. Twelve hours a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work while they were hauling?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, yeah. Ride the sleds right down. Every sled go down and ride down across my deep. Sand on the front end of it. A sack of cinders or sand scattered along as we got to go into it. Get off and go back and get another. I forget how many sleighs they had, but they had... They must have had ten or twelve teams, sleigh teams. Quite a bunch of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the sleighs like?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, they had big long runners and then bunks on, you know. They chained them on. Chained the logs over. Like they do on the railroad cars.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Binders and all.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But the teams would only bring one sleigh in, but the cats would take two. From the river up there, up hill.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Cats were more powerful than horses.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But they didn't have the cats around in the sleigh hall just down from the landing. Yeah, this from here to the river.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: About three and a half, four miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they do this real often, these sleigh halls?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No, that was the only one I've been on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story on why they did it just then?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, they bought the timber off in that school section, see. When their time was up, they either had to get the timber off or lose the timber. And so they made a road around through there to sleigh hall it out. Then a year or two after that, they built a railroad right through. But they'd lost the timber then. They got it off when they did. That's the only time they had left to get it to that winter. Either that or lose it. So they didn't want to lose it. It was nice to them to get a pine.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They just had so long a time after they bought it to get it out of there. I guess they figured on the railroad in the first place, but they didn't get it built.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, the sleigh hall wasn't too economical then, I get the idea. It wasn't as good as other...

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, I don't know. You've got quite a few logs. Sounds like it. Oh, yeah. Quite a few logs every day. Of course, they wasn't paying much money then. 30 cents for... I got, and the teams had got 2 cents and a half more an hour than I did. So they wasn't making too much money either. 12 hours a day. Oh, yeah. Of course, the lumber wasn't near high either. But there's lots of men there working on that. All together, the road monkeys and the teamsters and the loaders and skidders. They didn't take quite a crew.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: What was it like...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me some stories about prohibition?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Some of the things that... Some of the funny stories about guys drinking on the sleigh and stuff like that.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, I don't know too much about that. I never... If I have, I forgot them all.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: You told me a couple last time. I don't know if I can remember.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I ain't familiar with a lot of that stuff. A lot of things happened that I forgot all about, I guess. One of the ones you told me was about a dog, and I don't remember. It was too good. Oh, it was about a dog over here hunting whiskey bottles. Yeah. He'd go out and get them too. He'd be taking a hide one wherever he wanted to. And when he'd send him out, he'd come back with it. He belonged to Harry Tracy. The dog did. He had him trained. The engineer went out and hid one there, and Tracy sent him out, and pretty soon he came back with it. He said, that's the last one. I'll hide around down there.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, just from what I've heard about it, prohibition seems to not have been taken very seriously.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Pat Malone really liked his... Well, he used to watch the hall down there, and he'd take a drink. He didn't bother him much. I seen him come around the fence there one night. Some guy's ahead of him, and they stopped and got a drink. He'd come along, he stopped and got one, and he'd come on down and back up. I don't know whose bottle it was. It was dark there. You couldn't hardly see him up in the light. Old Pat had the flashlight. He was going along. I thought maybe he was going to try and catch them with it. All he wanted was a drink or something.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I guess he's dead a long time ago, too. Aw, he didn't bother anybody if they let him alone. I don't think.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Once in a while, I suppose he had to pick up somebody and show them he still copped.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: He was a pretty old man when I first met him.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Around Boulder there, he was. Oh, I thought he was 50 or 60, 50 or anyhow when he was in my first meeting. I don't know how old he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Pat asked Joe?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I used to. Last time I seen him was in a wire cage. They had to pick up a wire around it. He was in there bouncing all over, down there at Princeton. Last time I ever seen him. I seen him a lot of times before that. He used to work at the camps. I don't know if sometimes he'd work, sometimes he wouldn't.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: The boss would tell him, well, can you?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: He said, quit yourself. He said, we got too many bosses anyhow. He said, he was quick. Too many bosses anyhow, quit yourself. He said. They kept him on a long time. He was kind of a dirty looking old boss. I don't know how he. But the last time I seen him old, frilled down there, he was a mechanic. He had this pick up with a wire cage around, you know, on the top of it. I guess old Pat asked Joe wanted to come out with him and he loaded him in behind the end of the. Looked like a big bear in there the last time I seen him bouncing around. Yeah, he was quite an old potlifter. They called him old potlifter Joe.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Dick Benge was telling me last week that you could get pretty mad sometimes if a guy really got your goat.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Who was this?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dick Benge.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Told me that. I said, oh, I can't believe that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, Clay?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah. I don't know. I've been around Dick a lot too. I don't know. Well, because they wouldn't do what they were supposed to do. Trying to slough off on to me. I'd get kind of aggravated. I'd been held to timber pretty good. Of course, if I knew they was trying to slip it over on me, it would work a little different too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean, not do their job?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, you know, like that haulback you have to go to pull there. That's a two -man job. You get ahold of the front end, the other guy lay off behind on you unless you do all the pulling and stuff like that. Or get kind of aggravated and stuff like that. After that it was about so long.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I'd get behind then and I'd do the same with them. I'd let them pull along if they wanted to. If they couldn't pull, then I'd help them a little. I'd get even some way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Dick? Did you work with him very much?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, just in the camp I never worked right with him. He used to drive teams getting along. And then the last, I don't know, I guess the last several years, he scaled a lot, I think. Scaled logs, I don't know, some for pot lasses and I guess some for these little mills up here at Fernwood. He worked there quite a while, I know.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But most of the time when I was down around Princeton, he was driving a team in there skidding to the landings below the tracks.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Dick's a nice fella. He stopped in here a couple of weeks ago. His wife was going up to Fernwood after his grandkids. But they didn't stay long, they were in a hurry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Pete Olson? Pete up there in Bovill?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, is it Pete?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I thought it was. There's a Dick.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Dale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what's this mill here now?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Simon. There's one bull, it must be Dick. No, they're Dick.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Pete Olson, I was thinking I was sort of a, he had a real, like to do things his own way is what I had heard about him.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I know, I know. I thought maybe you knew. I knew all them boys, but I just couldn't think of his first name being Pete. His wife carries papers here. I don't know. If it's the one I think. There's a Pete Olson up there, used to be a bachelor. He lived right there in town. This guy, he's died. He's been dead. Oh, well, I knew him.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They found him dead in the ditch up there or something. Oh, speaking of dying, I was thinking about, I think it was Herschel Tribble said that you might know something about that Jess Dillman and Agnes Leiner murder over by Harvard. Well, I've heard about it. I don't know just what happened there. I've heard several different stories on it. Different ones tell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What have you heard?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, all I know about it is for sure he killed her up there. She used to live right here. Her brother lived here yet, right here on the hill. I have an eye that a woman could tell you more about it, how it was being identified. They was going together or something, and I don't know if something happened. Maybe it was in a mine or something or a camp up there.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yes, Bradford.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: He shot her and then shot himself, I guess.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: His brother was married to my sister, but I never did learn too much of just what did happen about it. Quite a few years ago. Yeah, well.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: So, I couldn't tell you too much. I don't know exactly what the argument was over, and how it turned out. But I know he shot her and then shot himself. That's about all I know about it. I didn't know him. I'd seen him, I guess, but I didn't really know him.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They got a sister in Proust, I think. Her name was Townes, Clarence Townes was my sister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you go over with me how some of these loaders went over? Would you go over with me how some of these loaders went over in these different situations?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, that one jumped the track. You see, the car jumped the track. The reason that one went over was going down to the road log, and it jumped. It just jumped the track, and that's the way she landed. It finally went on over, and the car stayed up.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, I wonder what would cause one to jump the track.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: It would just get going too fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, them old railroad tracks, them days, they wasn't fixed like they are now. They put tires in, and some of them didn't have balance in. They'd get the holes and the dips, and get going too fast, and them things. Them Marines are pretty heavy. This is the same one, I think. Same picture as that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: There is it. There's another one.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: This one I got two pictures of. There's another one I didn't take down.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: That one just pulled itself over in the skidway and couldn't get back up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this the one with the tank on top?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see, is it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, I see there's a water tank right on. I think most of them Marines had a tank on top, but that McGifford, the one that tipped over this one now, it had a way higher tank. There wasn't as much weight on behind. When that swung around, the water went back, I guess, over, and the feet tipped over. They wasn't pulling nothing when that one went over. I know. The log didn't even hang onto the log. The tongs didn't.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: And that's the one that tipped over down there too. Seems to me like there's two different tip -overs. This is the same as that. Yeah, it looks like about the same. Tucked at a different angle. Yeah.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: This here, I had the crew get on top of that one after they got... I used to know who all them was, but Spencer's one, Delos Randle, Francis O 'Keefe. I don't know, I tried to figure out the scale of that, but I can never think of any. Glenn Taylor is a fireman. That scaler is over here, and I know him well, and I can't think of his name, Sam.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Jack Dunnevin, that used to be a Czech scaler.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What caused you to stop working in the woods out here?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, I got tired of that old McGifford up there, and had to stay away from home most of the time. I got a chance to go to the state, and I said, the heck with it. I stayed home for it. It made it a lot better. I was home every night, and you know where you're going the next day. And they was only paying a dollar an hour when I quit in 44 for top load. Twelve hours a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job as top loader?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Placing the logs on the car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You directed where it would go?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah. You directed the guy that was doing...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: that was operating?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Then we'd have to pull them hooks out. Most of the time, they'd get stuck up there, and you had to be up there and pull them loose so they could get them back down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it very dangerous work?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, if you watch close enough, it wasn't. It wasn't quite as dangerous as Marion. You see these logs, and logs there where they're swinging them around, hooking me in here.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: See the tongs there?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: You got up, I'll hit a swing around with that log. You got to be on top of that car there and take the tongs out. If you don't swing it just about right, you might get hit with it. But I never did get hit with them. I was always watching pretty close. Once in a while, I wanted to fall out of the tongs or something, you know, hit the boom, and the log would come down. I was always lucky. Some of them wasn't. You told me you saw one... Most of them was on the ground that got hurt.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: How would they... Homer Cocker, he got hit on the legs. He was down there waiting to throw a chain over, and the log fell off on him. They smashed both of his legs, and they had to take him off, tuck him in pot -light. One day, he never come off one of these, and they cut his legs off. I guess the shock and stuff killed him. Then another one got killed up at Elk River. It was kind of slick and icy, and the log slid. He was trying to hold it, and there was another skidway right behind him, and pushed him right into that. Smashed him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he got hit by a log coming down the other skidway?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah, it slid off on the skidway, and he was trying to hold it with his hands, keeping them sliding. It shoved him right into that other skidway. Killed him, and then there was another one that got killed. I don't know just how it happened. His name was Parks, this young fella. I wasn't on that rig either. When he got killed, but I'd worked with him trying to get before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he get killed?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: With logs only. I don't know whether it fell out of the tongs, or whether it fell off on the skidway, or what happened.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: He died in St. Mary's, I think. He told me one other about a guy who got killed when you were working there.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, that was one. He was on loading the truck. He hollered me to look out down there. He was going to dump the little truck, the log down in there. He told me to get out of the road, and I told him to let her go. He tripped it up there in the log fella and killed him on the truck. It wasn't on the loader. When he knocked the fiddlers out, you know, he went on the truck there and dropped down on it. I guess he was hanging out too much. I don't remember what his name was. I didn't know the man too good. He just started trucking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that when the trucks had first started being used?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Yeah. That was up on the shoddy. Had a truck rode in there and starting to haul the truck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the change like there when they went to truck, using the trucks? What did the men think of it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, they'd haul them into these railroads, you see. You'd have a railroad a certain place, and then they'd have timber way off. They couldn't get a railroad into it. It'd be too steep or something. They'd make roads where these trucks come down around, and then they'd haul them into this railroad and then load them up in cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think it was an improvement to do this?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, I don't know. It looked to me like awful expensive, but it was a poor rig for the country. After they quit the horses, they tore down more timber than the truck, I think. Them cats going up through a pond around, tearing up the little stuff. The horses, you know, they just had skid trails with them. Didn't tear up much.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: I hear they're talking about going back to horses quite a bit. They got one guy up here now skidding with horses someplace. I think out of Clarkia, up this side Hittin' Creek or someplace in there. That way they don't tear down all the young stuff.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But you take these skidders and stuff, you know, they make a road about ever so far around the hillside. You know, big high banks and it all washes and just tears the ground up and ruins it, I might say. You can go anyplace up in them hills. You see roads all around the hillside where they go.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: And they skid them with a jammer and they tear down the rest of what they don't get when they fall the trees. They don't need to tear down the rest of those skidded out pole lakes.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: No, I think they can do a lot different with it. Of course this is faster and don't take near as many men.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: There aren't as many men to give work to, I guess, then.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They used to have all the way from 100 to 150 men, you know, in one of them camps. Now that they got probably 50, not that many. Takes the place of the whole works.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you skid with a horse?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Oh, you'd go back, I don't know, some of them go back 12, 15, 100 feet. You know, then they'd make a railroad into another place. Downhill, you know, you'd skid them down quite a ways. If it wasn't, they'd move them over onto another track. And the drawers would go up, you know, and they'd build a railroad right in the drawers and skid to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think putting in a railroad compares with putting in a road? Putting in the track, is it a lot more work?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, yeah, it'd be more work to put the track in. I'd say, I don't know. Of course, nowadays, the bulldozers they got wouldn't be much of a job to put the railroad in either. But just laying the track, they had machines to do that with, you know. Motors would swing the rails and ties around, they'd throw them out there and scatter them out, and then they'd lay the rails on them, spike them down, keep them going. I was getting the road bids the first place I was, it'd be the worst job. Of course, the dozers now, they're doing the road pretty fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that you'd worked on the railroad? Railroad? Did you ever work on it?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Not on the mainline, no, just these...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The shades?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: The shades and stuff, just loading them. They was all railroaded in the woods, you know, where we loaded them. We used to deck them with horses and them big decks, put them, you know, skid them up and deck them way higher. Then we'd go along spring, load them out.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: They always had lots of logs on hand, and they had horses. Of course, they had an awful lot of crews, too. Lots of camps. Now they ain't got no camps. You got to stay home or take you a trail or a camp or something to camp in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the most important thing you learned in camp?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: You know, I used to stay in camp, board in camp. That is the wood screws. Of course, we was running all over different places. We'd stay in one camp one night and probably another camp another night. Maybe stay at home one night. It just depends on where you're at. Long time no see.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: But them camps used to get pretty old, too. Same old thing all the time, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that looking back on it that Potlatch managed the woods about as well as they could have? Or the things that they could have done that would have been better for the country?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Well, I don't know. You know, they had to go over the card in the way everything else goes. Like this machinery and stuff. If they don't buy that, these outfits don't buy their stuff. I suppose they had to stay up with it. As far as the logging was concerned, there was a lot more work for people than had horses. It didn't tear the ground up like they do, and I don't think they wasted nearly as much timber as they do. But the guys, they had to go along with the bigger outfits, too, I suppose.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Trying new stuff.

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Like that implant up there they had, you know. They had to pull them cards up with a donkey and let the loaders down with donkeys. And then after they found out that didn't work too good, they built a railroad right back in where they were getting the timber from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that at?

speaker: Clay Gustin
words: Clarkia. I went up that one strong flat tire, but I never come down. That thing was just, you had to lay down and hang on to your hands to keep it sliding off and that thing. How they ever got, they put cables, big cables over the logs and cinched them up and let them down with a donkey down there.
